01_hello.title: Hello, world!

q1.question: What does this program print? (Type the exact output.)

q2.question: What is println! ?
options: A function
A macro
A trait
A keyword

q3.question: In `println!("Hello, {name}!")`, what does `{name}` do?
options: Creates a new variable called `name`
Prints the braces literally
Inserts the value of `name` into the output
Calls a method on `name`

02_variables.title: Variables and mutability
02_variables.description:
`let` creates a binding.

```rust
let y = 10;
```

This means: make a variable named `y` and bind it to `10`.

```rust
let mut y = 10;
y = 11;
```

`mut` allows reassignment. Without `mut`, `y = 11;` is an error.

```rust
let y: i32 = 10;
```

`:` introduces a type annotation. Here, `y` is explicitly marked as `i32`.

```rust
let y = y + 1;
```

This does not mutate the old `y`. It creates a new `y` by shadowing the previous one.

q2.question: If you write `let y: i32 = 10;` without `mut`, which extra line still compiles?
options: let y = y + 1;
y.set(11);
explanations: This tries to assign a new value to `y`, but `y` is immutable because it was not declared with `mut`.
This is also a mutation. `+=` still changes the existing variable, so it needs `mut`.
This works because it creates a new variable named `y` that shadows the old one instead of mutating it.
`i32` does not have a `.set(...)` method, so this fails before mutability even matters.
q2.answerReason: The correct choice is `let y = y + 1;` because it shadows the original binding with a new one.

q3.question: What is happening in `let y = y + 1;`?
options: The old `y` is mutated in place
A new `y` shadows the old one
Rust automatically adds `mut`
The type of `y` changes to `usize`

`return` is illegal in Rust

Rust ignores the semicolon
The block no longer returns `a + b` as its value

08_enums.title: Enums and match
08_enums.description:
An enum lets one value have several possible shapes.

```rust
enum Direction {
    Left,
    Right,
}
```

Use `match` to handle each variant.

```rust
match dir {
    Direction::Left => println!("L"),
    Direction::Right => println!("R"),
}
```

`match` must cover every variant.

q2.question: Which statement about `match` on an enum is true?
options: Enums can't carry data
match needs exhaustiveness only for primitive types
match must cover every variant
All variants must share the same data type

q3.question: What does one `match` arm usually handle?
options: A single line comment
A function call only
A type annotation
One enum variant or pattern

09_structs.title: Structs and methods
09_structs.description:
A Rust `struct` is the closest thing to class-like data: it groups fields, but it does not bundle methods inside the type body.

```rust
struct User {
    name: String,
    age: u32,
}
```

Methods live in a separate `impl` block.

```rust
impl User {
    fn is_adult(&self) -> bool {
        self.age >= 18
    }
}
```

A useful mental map is:

```text
class-like data         -> struct
class-like methods      -> impl
interface-like behavior -> trait
variant type            -> enum
```

But the mapping is not exact. Rust structs participate in ownership rules. Assigning a struct usually moves it unless the type implements `Copy`.

q2.question: What does an `impl` block do for a struct?
options: Attaches methods and associated functions
Hides all fields from outside code
Replaces the struct's fields
Runs at program startup

q3.question: Which statement about assigning a struct value is true by default?
options: It always deep-copies every field
It usually moves the value unless the type implements `Copy`
It converts the struct into a trait object
It is only allowed inside an `impl` block

16_traits_basic.title: Traits

q2.question: What does `impl Greet for Person` do?
options: Defines a free function called `greet`
Provides the `Greet` trait's methods for `Person`
Removes the `Greet` trait from `Person`

options: They make all types have the same fields

17_traits_default.title: Traits: default methods

q2.question: Why does `Dog` print `dog: ...`?
options: Both Cat and Dog override `sound()`
Dog uses the default `sound()` because it doesn't override it
Default methods cannot be overridden
Cat doesn't implement the trait

q3.question: Why are default trait methods useful in practice?
options: They force every type to use the same exact implementation
They remove the need for trait implementations entirely
They let a trait provide shared behavior while still allowing overrides
They make traits work only for enums

18_traits_derive.title: Traits: derive
18_traits_derive.description:
`derive` asks Rust to generate trait implementations for you.

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Copy, Clone, PartialEq)]
struct Point {
    x: i32,
    y: i32,
}
```

This matters for value-like structs. With `Copy`, assigning `p1` into `p2` duplicates the bits, so both variables stay usable.

```rust
let p1 = Point { x: 1, y: 2 };
let p2 = p1;
```

Without `Copy`, that assignment would move the value and `p1` could not be used afterward. `Clone` is the explicit duplicate operation when automatic copying is not available or not desired.

q2.question: What does `#[derive(Debug, Copy, Clone, PartialEq)]` do?
options: Auto-generates implementations of those traits
Turns every field into a reference
Replaces the struct's fields
Runs at runtime

q3.question: Why can `p1` still be used after `let p2 = p1;` in this lesson?
options: Because `PartialEq` keeps the old binding alive
Because `Debug` prevents moves
Because the struct implements `Copy`, so assignment copies instead of moving
Because all structs in Rust are copied by default

19_generics_basic.title: Generics

q2.question: Which is the correct syntax for a bounded generic?
options: T must be a number
T = PartialOrd + Copy
T: PartialOrd + Copy
T :: PartialOrd, Copy

q3.question: Why does `largest<T: PartialOrd + Copy>` need `Copy` in this example?
options: So Rust can sort values automatically
So the function can return `a` or `b` by value without moving out of borrowed data
Because every generic type must implement `Copy`
Because `PartialOrd` only works together with `Copy`

20_lifetimes_basic.title: Lifetimes

q2.question: What does the `'a` in `fn longest<'a>(x: &'a str, y: &'a str) -> &'a str` mean?
options: 'a is a runtime variable
The returned reference lives at least as long as both inputs
'a is required for every function
'a only applies to Strings

q3.question: What problem are lifetimes solving here?
options: They let Rust track how long returned references may safely point to input data
They make the function run longer
They allocate strings on the heap
They replace ownership with garbage collection

21_lifetimes_struct.title: Lifetimes in structs
21_lifetimes_struct.description:
A struct that stores a reference needs a lifetime parameter.

```rust
struct Borrower<'a> {
    name: &'a str,
}
```

Without `'a`, Rust would not know how long `name` is allowed to live.

q2.question: Which line below has the missing lifetime that prevents this from compiling?

q3.question: Why must `Borrower` declare a lifetime parameter?
options: Because the struct stores a reference and Rust must know how long that reference may live
Because every struct in Rust needs a lifetime
Because `String` always requires `'static`
Because structs cannot hold fields without trait bounds

22_result_basic.title: Result and match
22_result_basic.description:
`Result<T, E>` represents success or failure.

```rust
let value: Result<i32, &str> = Ok(42);
```

Use `match` to handle both cases.

```rust
match value {
    Ok(n) => println!("{n}"),
    Err(e) => println!("error: {e}"),
}
```

q2.question: Which is true about Result?
options: It's an enum with Ok and Err variants
It's a struct
Err always panics
match cannot pattern-match Result

q3.question: Why is `Result` preferred over panicking for recoverable failures?
options: It forces the caller to handle or propagate the failure explicitly
It makes the compiler run faster
It removes the need for return types
It guarantees the program never exits early

23_option_basic.title: Option and unwrap_or

q2.question: What does `unwrap_or("none")` do when called on `None`?
options: Panics
Returns the empty string
Returns the default "none"
Calls the function again

q3.question: When would you choose `Option<T>` instead of `Result<T, E>`?
options: When the absence of a value is expected and you do not need an error message
When you need to store two successful values
When parsing integers from text
Only when working with traits

24_error_propagation.title: Error propagation with ?

q2.question: Order what happens when `parse_and_double("21")` is called, in execution order.
options: Try to parse "21" as i32
Parsing succeeds; `?` unwraps Ok(21) into n
Multiply 21 by 2 to get 42
Wrap 42 in Ok and return

q3.question: What happens if the parse step returns `Err` before multiplication?
options: The function keeps going with `0`
The error is ignored
The `?` returns that error early from the function
Rust retries the parse automatically

25_panic_unwrap.title: panic, unwrap, expect

q2.question: What happens if `value.unwrap()` is called when `value` is `None`?

q3.question: Why is `expect("message")` often better than plain `unwrap()` during debugging?
options: It never panics
It converts `None` into `Some`
It makes the code async-safe

26_async_basic.title: async / await

q2.question: What does calling an `async fn` actually do?

q3.question: Why do we need something like `block_on` in this example?

q2.question: Where does Box<T> store its data?

On the heap
In thread-local storage

q3.question: Why would you choose `Box<T>` over a plain `T`?
options: To move the value to the heap or to build recursive structures

To bypass ownership checks

28_rc_basic.title: Smart pointers: Rc

q2.question: Which best describes Rc<T>?

q3.question: Why is `Rc<T>` not used across threads?
options: Because its reference counting is not thread-safe
Because it can only store integers

q3.question: What extra risk comes with `RefCell<T>` compared with normal borrowing?
options: It disables type checking
It can panic at runtime if borrow rules are broken

30_macros_basic.title: Macros: built-in

q2.question: When do macros run?
options: When the binary starts up
On every function call
At compile time, expanding into code
Lazily, on first use

q3.question: Why are macros useful for things like `println!` and `vec!`?
options: They can accept flexible syntax and expand into regular Rust before compilation finishes
They run faster than machine code
They replace the type system
They only work in debug mode

options: A regular function
A declarative macro

q3.question: How does a declarative macro decide what code to generate?
options: By matching the input against patterns and expanding the matching rule
By executing the code at runtime first
By compiling a separate crate automatically
By reading values from stdin

32_unsafe_basic.title: Unsafe Rust

q2.question: Which is true about `unsafe { }`?
options: Inside it you can dereference raw pointers
It disables the type system
It makes Rust as memory-safe as Java
Raw pointers are checked at runtime

q3.question: What does `unsafe` actually change?
options: It turns off all borrow checking forever
It allows specific operations the compiler cannot verify, while the rest of Rust rules still apply
It makes raw pointers safe automatically
It removes the need for types

33_vec_push.title: Vectors and push

q2.question: Fill in the method used to append `3` to the vector.

q3.question: Why does the vector need `mut` before calling `push`?
options: Because `push` changes the vector's contents and possibly its length
Because vectors are immutable forever in Rust
Because `push` returns a new vector
Because all heap values require `mut`

34_option_match.title: Option with match guards
34_option_match.description:
`match` arms can use guards.

```rust
match value {
    Some(n) if n > 10 => "big",
    Some(_) => "small",
    None => "none",
}
```

The `if n > 10` part is a guard that adds an extra condition.

q2.question: Why does `Some(25)` print `big`?
options: Because every Some value is treated as big
Because the `Some(n) if n > 10` guard matches
Because match checks None first
Because Option automatically unwraps values over 10

q3.question: What is the benefit of a match guard here?
options: It lets one pattern split into more precise cases without nesting another `if`
It removes the need for `Some` and `None`
It turns `Option` into `Result`
It makes the match run at compile time

q2.question: Order what happens in this program.
options: Create an Arc holding a Mutex with 0 inside
Clone the Arc so two owners point to the same Mutex
Lock the Mutex and add 2 to the inner value
Lock again to print the final count

q3.question: Why are `Arc` and `Mutex` often paired together?
options: Because `Arc` shares ownership and `Mutex` protects mutable access to shared data
Because both are required for string formatting
Because `Arc` provides mutation and `Mutex` provides cloning
Because neither can be used alone

q2.question: The snippet below has one line that won't compile. Which line is it?

options: Because `enumerate()` returns a closure
Because each iteration item is a tuple of index and value
Because Rust loops always need parentheses
Because arrays cannot be iterated without tuple syntax

37_thread_basic.title: Threads: spawn and join

q2.question: Why does the program call `handle.join().unwrap()`?
options: To start the thread lazily
To make the closure mutable
To wait for the spawned thread to finish
To clone the thread handle

q3.question: What does `thread::spawn` return?
options: A borrowed reference to the thread body
A `JoinHandle` that can be used to wait for the thread
A `Mutex` protecting the thread
A `Result<String, String>`

38_mutex_basic.title: Locks: Mutex

q2.question: Order what happens in this program.
options: Create a mutex holding `0`
Lock the mutex and add `5` to the inner value
Lock it again to print the final count

q3.question: Why is a `MutexGuard` useful?
options: It keeps the lock held only while the guarded value is in scope
It copies the protected data into a new thread
It removes the need to handle lock poisoning
It automatically converts shared data into `Rc<T>`

41_trait_objects.title: Trait Objects and Dynamic Dispatch

q1.question: What does this program print? (Type the exact output.)

q2.question: What does `&dyn Draw` represent?
options: A reference to any value that implements `Draw`, using dynamic dispatch.
A macro that creates buttons.
A mutable borrow of every type at once.
explanations: Correct. The concrete type is hidden behind trait-object behavior.
It is not a macro.
That is not what trait objects mean.
q2.answerReason: Trait objects provide behavior-based polymorphism at runtime.

q3.question: How is this different from a generic `T: Draw` parameter?
options: Generics always allocate on the heap.
Trait objects can only be used in tests.
Generics preserve compile-time concrete type information, while trait objects erase it behind a runtime interface.
explanations: Generics do not always allocate.
Trait objects are a normal production feature.
Correct. This is the key design distinction.
q3.answerReason: The trait-object versus generic choice changes dispatch and what type information stays available.

44_interior_mutability.title: Interior Mutability Tradeoffs
44_interior_mutability.description:
Interior mutability allows mutation through shared ownership patterns by moving some checks to runtime or abstraction boundaries.

```rust
let value = RefCell::new(1);
```

This is useful, but it changes where borrow-rule enforcement happens.

q1.question: What does this program print? (Type the exact output.)

q2.question: Why is `RefCell<T>` a tradeoff?
options: Because it moves some borrow enforcement from compile time to runtime.
Because it removes all ownership reasoning.
Because it is always thread-safe shared state.
explanations: Correct. Flexibility comes with different enforcement timing.
Ownership reasoning still matters.
`RefCell` is not the thread-safe shared-state default.
q2.answerReason: Interior mutability increases flexibility by changing where correctness is checked.

q3.question: What is the main design lesson?
options: Runtime borrow checks are always better.
Compile-time checks no longer matter in Rust.
Extra flexibility should be used deliberately because it changes failure modes and API expectations.
explanations: That is too absolute.
Compile-time checks still matter a lot.
Correct. The tradeoff is about where guarantees come from.
q3.answerReason: Interior mutability is powerful, but it is not a free replacement for simpler ownership models.

46_crate_boundaries.title: Crate Boundaries and Architecture

q1.question: What does this program print? (Type the exact output.)

q2.question: Why do crate boundaries matter beyond build organization?
options: Because only crates can contain traits.
Because modules cannot be tested.
Because they help encode API ownership, dependency direction, and isolation boundaries.
explanations: Traits are not limited to crates that way.
Modules can be tested.
Correct. Package boundaries are part of system structure.
q2.answerReason: Crate boundaries influence coupling and what invariants are enforced where.

q3.question: What is the main architecture benefit?
options: Only faster formatting.
A clearer public surface and better control over internal coupling.
Automatic removal of all unsafe code.
explanations: Formatting is unrelated.
Correct. Structure helps keep large systems disciplined.
Unsafe code is not removed automatically.
q3.answerReason: Good boundaries make large codebases easier to reason about and evolve.

40_copy_clone_value.title: Copy, Clone, and Value Semantics
40_copy_clone_value.description:
A `Copy` type behaves more like a traditional value type because assignment duplicates it implicitly.

```rust
#[derive(Copy, Clone)]
struct Point { x: i32, y: i32 }
```

Without `Copy`, moving the value would make the old binding unusable.

q1.question: What does this program print? (Type the exact output.)

q2.question: Why can `p1` still be used after `let p2 = p1;`?
options: Because structs are always shared by reference.
Because `Clone` runs automatically on every assignment.
Because `Point` implements `Copy`, so the assignment duplicates the value implicitly.
explanations: That is not how ordinary struct assignment works here.
`Clone` is explicit; `Copy` is the implicit behavior.
Correct. `Copy` gives assignment-like duplication semantics.
q2.answerReason: `Copy` changes assignment from a move into an implicit duplication.

q3.question: Why does `Copy` often feel like value semantics?
options: Because `Copy` values are not stored in memory.
Because passing or assigning them does not transfer exclusive ownership away from the old binding.
Because `Copy` disables type checking.
explanations: All values are still stored somewhere in memory.
Correct. The original binding remains usable after assignment-like operations.
Type checking still applies.
q3.answerReason: Implicit duplication is why `Copy` often matches value-type intuition.

42_send_sync.title: Send, Sync, and Thread Safety
42_send_sync.description:
`Send` and `Sync` describe what ownership and shared references may safely cross thread boundaries.

```rust
let data = Arc::new(String::from("shared"));
```

These traits shape what concurrent Rust designs are legal.

q1.question: What does this program print? (Type the exact output.)

q2.question: Why is `Arc` used here instead of plain `Rc`?
options: Because `Rc` is always mutable.
Because `Arc` is thread-safe shared ownership, while `Rc` is not for cross-thread sharing.
Because `Arc` removes the need for ownership.
explanations: `Rc` being always mutable is false.
Correct. `Arc` is the thread-safe shared-ownership choice.
Ownership still exists.
q2.answerReason: Shared ownership across threads needs thread-safe reference counting.

q3.question: What do `Send` and `Sync` help express?
options: Whether ownership or shared references can cross thread boundaries safely.
Only how TCP packets are routed.
How the formatter aligns code.
explanations: Correct. They encode thread-safety properties.
Networking is unrelated.
Formatting is unrelated.
q3.answerReason: These traits are Rust’s vocabulary for thread-safety guarantees.

45_async_blocking.title: Blocking Work Inside Async
45_async_blocking.description:
Async executors rely on tasks yielding cooperatively.

```rust
// blocking work here can stall executor threads
```

A blocking call in the wrong place can damage latency and throughput assumptions.

q1.question: What does this program print? (Type the exact output.)

q2.question: Why is blocking work inside async a design problem?
options: Because async code cannot call ordinary functions.
Because blocking can starve executor threads and hurt latency or throughput.
Because blocked tasks become compile errors automatically.
explanations: Async code can still call ordinary functions.
Correct. Blocking undermines cooperative progress.
This is a runtime architecture problem, not an automatic compile error.
q2.answerReason: Async structure depends on tasks not monopolizing executor threads with blocking work.

q3.question: What is the broader lesson?
options: Concurrency model assumptions matter when choosing APIs and runtime behavior.
Only syntax matters in async Rust.
Blocking always improves fairness.
explanations: Correct. Runtime model and API choice are connected.
Syntax alone is not enough.
Blocking does not automatically improve fairness.
q3.answerReason: Architecture choices have to respect the executor’s concurrency model.

39_shadowing_basics.title: Shadowing Versus Mutation

q1.question: What does this program print? (Type the exact output.)

q2.question: What is happening in `let x = x + 1;`?
options: The old binding is mutated in place.
A new binding shadows the previous one.
`x` becomes a macro.
explanations: Mutation would require a mutable binding assignment.
Correct. Shadowing creates a fresh binding.
`x` is still a variable binding.
q2.answerReason: Shadowing reuses the name for a new binding rather than mutating the old one.

q3.question: Why does this distinction matter?
options: Because ownership, type changes, and immutability reasoning depend on whether you shadow or mutate.
Because Rust cannot print mutable values.
Because shadowing only works in tests.
explanations: Correct. The semantics are different even when the name is reused.
Printing is unrelated.
Shadowing is a normal language feature.
q3.answerReason: Shadowing and mutation affect how you reason about the binding and its lifetime.

43_pin_basics.title: Pin and Stable Location
43_pin_basics.description:
`Pin` exists for abstractions that rely on values not being moved after they are pinned.

```rust
let pinned = Pin::new(Box::new(value));
```

This matters for some async and self-referential patterns.

q1.question: What does this program print? (Type the exact output.)

q2.question: Why does `Pin` exist?
options: Because pinned values always run faster.
Because the stack cannot hold strings.
Because some abstractions rely on stable memory location for soundness.
explanations: `Pin` is not a universal speed trick.
Strings can be held by values normally.
Correct. Stable location is the point.
q2.answerReason: Pin protects designs that would be unsound if values were moved freely.

q3.question: Where does this show up often?
options: Only in comments.
In async state machines and other move-sensitive abstractions.
Only when printing macros are used.
explanations: Comments are unrelated.
Correct. Async internals are a common case.
Macros are unrelated.
q3.answerReason: Pinning becomes relevant when movement itself can break invariants.
